title: Container With Most Water
description: Find two lines that together with x-axis form container holding maximum water

examples.explanation: Initialize max_area = 0. Check all pairs: i=0 (height=1): j=1 area=min(1,8)*1=1, max_area=1; j=2 area=min(1,6)*2=2, max_area=2; j=3 area=min(1,2)*3=3, max_area=3; j=4 area=min(1,5)*4=4, max_area=4; j=5 area=min(1,4)*5=5, max_area=5; j=6 area=min(1,8)*6=6, max_area=6; j=7 area=min(1,3)*7=7, max_area=7; j=8 area=min(1,7)*8=8, max_area=8. i=1 (height=8): j=2 area=min(8,6)*1=6, max_area=8; j=3 area=min(8,2)*2=4, max_area=8; j=4 area=min(8,5)*3=15, max_area=15; j=5 area=min(8,4)*4=16, max_area=16; j=6 area=min(8,8)*5=40, max_area=40; j=7 area=min(8,3)*6=18, max_area=40; j=8 area=min(8,7)*7=49, max_area=49. i=2 (height=6): j=3 area=min(6,2)*1=2, max_area=49; j=4 area=min(6,5)*2=10, max_area=49; j=5 area=min(6,4)*3=12, max_area=49; j=6 area=min(6,8)*4=24, max_area=49; j=7 area=min(6,3)*5=15, max_area=49; j=8 area=min(6,7)*6=36, max_area=49. Continue checking remaining pairs (i=3 to i=7), all areas ≤ 49. Final max_area = 49.
Initialize max_area = 0. Only one pair: i=0 (height=1), j=1 (height=1). Calculate area = min(1,1) * (1-0) = 1 * 1 = 1. Update max_area = 1. Return 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check every possible pair of lines by iterating through all combinations of i and j where i < j. For each pair, calculate the area as min(height[i], height[j]) * (j - i) and track the maximum.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Container With Most Water (Brute Force O(n²))
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking all possible pairs of lines to find maximum water container area.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the Container With Most Water problem by systematically examining every possible pair of vertical lines to find the pair that forms the container holding the maximum amount of water. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to find the best pair among all possibilities, we can simply try all combinations. For each pair of indices (i, j) where i < j, we calculate the area as min(height[i], height[j]) × (j - i). The minimum height determines the water level (since water would overflow from the shorter side), while the distance between indices gives the width. We maintain a running maximum and update it whenever we find a larger area. This approach is guaranteed to find the optimal solution because it explores the entire solution space. The algorithm starts with max_area = 0, then uses nested loops: the outer loop iterates i from 0 to n-2, and the inner loop iterates j from i+1 to n-1. For each pair, it calculates current_area = min(height[i], height[j]) × (j - i) and updates max_area if current_area is larger. Time complexity is O(n²) because we have n(n-1)/2 pairs to check. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, correct solution. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to use min() and instead using max() or average, 2) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, 3) Not initializing max_area properly (should be 0, not negative infinity for non-negative heights). Real code pattern: def maxArea(height): n = len(height); max_area = 0; for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): area = min(height[i], height[j]) * (j - i); max_area = max(max_area, area); return max_area.

examples.trace: Initialize max_area = 0. Check all pairs: i=0 (height=1): j=1 area=min(1,8)*1=1, max_area=1; j=2 area=min(1,6)*2=2, max_area=2; j=3 area=min(1,2)*3=3, max_area=3; j=4 area=min(1,5)*4=4, max_area=4; j=5 area=min(1,4)*5=5, max_area=5; j=6 area=min(1,8)*6=6, max_area=6; j=7 area=min(1,3)*7=7, max_area=7; j=8 area=min(1,7)*8=8, max_area=8. i=1 (height=8): j=2 area=min(8,6)*1=6, max_area=8; j=3 area=min(8,2)*2=4, max_area=8; j=4 area=min(8,5)*3=15, max_area=15; j=5 area=min(8,4)*4=16, max_area=16; j=6 area=min(8,8)*5=40, max_area=40; j=7 area=min(8,3)*6=18, max_area=40; j=8 area=min(8,7)*7=49, max_area=49. i=2 (height=6): j=3 area=min(6,2)*1=2, max_area=49; j=4 area=min(6,5)*2=10, max_area=49; j=5 area=min(6,4)*3=12, max_area=49; j=6 area=min(6,8)*4=24, max_area=49; j=7 area=min(6,3)*5=15, max_area=49; j=8 area=min(6,7)*6=36, max_area=49. Continue checking remaining pairs (i=3 to i=7), all areas ≤ 49. Final max_area = 49.
Initialize max_area = 0. Only one pair: i=0 (height=1), j=1 (height=1). Calculate area = min(1,1) * (1-0) = 1 * 1 = 1. Update max_area = 1. Return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Calculate Area for a Pair of Lines
ladders.desc: Compute the water container area formed by two vertical lines at given indices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force solution: given two indices i and j (with i < j) and an array of heights, calculate how much water the container formed by these two lines can hold. The intuition comes from the physical constraints: water is limited by the shorter line (it would overflow over the shorter side), and the width is simply the horizontal distance between the lines. Therefore, area = min(height[i], height[j]) × (j - i). This formula captures the rectangular area: width × height, where height is the minimum of the two line heights. The algorithm is simple: take the two heights, find their minimum, multiply by the distance between indices. Time complexity is O(1) for a single calculation. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds directly into the full problem (L0) as the core computation performed for each pair. Common mistakes: 1) Using max instead of min (would overestimate area), 2) Forgetting absolute value for distance (though i < j ensures positive), 3) Using average height. Real code pattern: def calculate_area(height, i, j): return min(height[i], height[j]) * (j - i).

examples.trace: height[i] = height[1] = 8, height[j] = height[8] = 7. min(8,7) = 7. j - i = 8 - 1 = 7. Area = 7 × 7 = 49.
height[i] = height[0] = 4, height[j] = height[4] = 4. min(4,4) = 4. j - i = 4 - 0 = 4. Area = 4 × 4 = 16.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Pairs with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Generate all unique pairs of indices (i, j) where i < j using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is to systematically generate every possible pair of lines to check. This is achieved using nested loops: the outer loop variable i goes from 0 to n-2, and for each i, the inner loop variable j goes from i+1 to n-1. This ensures we consider all combinations exactly once without repetition (since i < j). The intuition is combinatorial: to find the best pair, we must examine all n choose 2 = n(n-1)/2 possibilities. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Get n = length of height array. 2) For i from 0 to n-2: 3) For j from i+1 to n-1: process pair (i, j). This generates pairs in order: (0,1), (0,2), ..., (0,n-1), (1,2), (1,3), ..., (n-2, n-1). Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the pairs for which area is calculated, and to L0 as the main control flow. Common mistakes: 1) Starting inner loop at i instead of i+1 (would include i=j, invalid), 2) Going to n instead of n-1 in loops (index out of bounds), 3) Using while loops incorrectly. Real code pattern: for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): # process pair (i, j).

examples.trace: n = 3. Outer loop i from 0 to 1: i=0: inner loop j from 1 to 2: j=1 → pair (0,1); j=2 → pair (0,2). i=1: inner loop j from 2 to 2: j=2 → pair (1,2). Total 3 pairs.

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Minimum of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Determine the smaller of two given numbers.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential because the water container's height is limited by the shorter line. Finding the minimum of two numbers is a fundamental operation used in the area calculation. The intuition is simple: between two lines, water can only fill up to the height of the shorter one before spilling over. The algorithm: compare the two numbers using a conditional: if a < b, return a; else return b. Alternatively, use min(a, b) function if available. Time complexity is O(1), space O(1). This connects to L2 as part of the area calculation for each pair. Common mistakes: 1) Returning max instead of min, 2) Not handling equal numbers properly (should return either, as min is same), 3) Overcomplicating with unnecessary operations. Real code pattern: def min_of_two(a, b): return a if a < b else b.

examples.trace: Compare 8 and 7: 8 < 7? No. So return b=7.
Compare 4 and 4: 4 < 4? No (they are equal). So return b=4 (or a=4, both correct).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Multiply Two Integers
ladders.desc: Compute the product of two integer numbers.
ladders.explanation: Multiplication is a basic arithmetic operation used to calculate area: area = height × width. In this context, after finding the minimum height, we multiply it by the distance between indices to get the rectangular area. The intuition is geometric: area of a rectangle is base times height. The algorithm is straightforward: given two integers a and b, compute a × b. This can be done using the * operator in programming. Time complexity is O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as part of the area calculation. Common mistakes: 1) Using addition instead of multiplication, 2) Overflow with large numbers (though in this problem, heights and n are typically within reasonable bounds), 3) Not using parentheses correctly in expressions. Real code pattern: product = a * b.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve a value from an array using its position (index).
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: in programming, arrays (or lists) store multiple values, and each value can be accessed using its index, which is an integer representing its position. Indexing typically starts at 0: the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. The intuition is like house numbering on a street. To access an element, use syntax like array[index]. For example, if height = [1,8,6], then height[0] gives 1, height[1] gives 8. This is fundamental because in the Container problem, we need to get the height of lines at specific indices i and j. The algorithm: given an array and an index, return the value at that position. Time complexity is O(1) for direct access. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L4 as we need heights to multiply. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (starting at 1), 2) Accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., index 3 in array of size 3), 3) Confusing index with value. Real code pattern: value = height[i].

examples.trace: Array indices: 0→4, 1→3, 2→2, 3→1, 4→4. Access index 4: height[4] = 4.

approaches.name: Two Pointer Greedy O(n)
approaches.summary: Start with pointers at both ends of the array. At each step, calculate the area and move the pointer pointing to the shorter line inward, since moving the taller line cannot increase the area. Continue until pointers meet.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Container With Most Water with Two Pointer Greedy
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding maximum water container area using two pointers from both ends.
ladders.explanation: The Container With Most Water problem requires finding two lines in an array of heights that form a container holding the maximum amount of water with the x-axis. The two pointer greedy approach solves this optimally in O(n) time by starting with pointers at both ends and moving the pointer with the shorter height inward at each step. The intuition is that the area is limited by the shorter line, so moving the taller line inward cannot increase the area—it can only decrease width while height remains constrained by the shorter line. By always moving the shorter pointer, we explore all potential maximum configurations efficiently. The algorithm initializes left=0, right=n-1, and max_area=0. While left < right, calculate area = min(height[left], height[right]) * (right-left), update max_area, then move the pointer with smaller height inward. Continue until pointers meet. Time complexity is O(n) since each pointer traverses the array once. Space complexity is O(1) using only pointers and variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete greedy decision logic. Common mistakes include moving the wrong pointer (always move shorter), not updating max_area correctly, or using brute force O(n²). Real code pattern: def maxArea(height): left, right, max_area = 0, len(height)-1, 0; while left < right: area = min(height[left], height[right]) * (right-left); max_area = max(max_area, area); if height[left] < height[right]: left += 1 else: right -= 1; return max_area.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0 (height=1), right=8 (height=7), area=min(1,7)*8=1*8=8, max_area=8, move left (shorter) to left=1. Step 2: left=1 (8), right=8 (7), area=min(8,7)*7=7*7=49, max_area=49, move right (shorter) to right=7. Step 3: left=1 (8), right=7 (3), area=min(8,3)*6=3*6=18, max_area=49, move right to right=6. Step 4: left=1 (8), right=6 (8), area=min(8,8)*5=8*5=40, max_area=49, move left or right (equal), choose left to left=2. Step 5: left=2 (6), right=6 (8), area=min(6,8)*4=6*4=24, max_area=49, move left to left=3. Step 6: left=3 (2), right=6 (8), area=min(2,8)*3=2*3=6, max_area=49, move left to left=4. Step 7: left=4 (5), right=6 (8), area=min(5,8)*2=5*2=10, max_area=49, move left to left=5. Step 8: left=5 (4), right=6 (8), area=min(4,8)*1=4*1=4, max_area=49, move left to left=6. Step 9: left=6, right=6, stop. Final max_area=49.

Step 1: left=0 (4), right=4 (4), area=min(4,4)*4=4*4=16, max_area=16, move left or right (equal), choose left to left=1. Step 2: left=1 (3), right=4 (4), area=min(3,4)*3=3*3=9, max_area=16, move left to left=2. Step 3: left=2 (2), right=4 (4), area=min(2,4)*2=2*2=4, max_area=16, move left to left=3. Step 4: left=3 (1), right=4 (4), area=min(1,4)*1=1*1=1, max_area=16, move left to left=4. Step 5: left=4, right=4, stop. Final max_area=16.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Greedy Pointer Movement Decision
ladders.desc: Implement the decision logic to move the shorter pointer inward at each step.
ladders.explanation: The key sub-routine in the two pointer approach is deciding which pointer to move at each iteration. Given left and right pointers with corresponding heights, we must move the pointer pointing to the shorter line inward. The intuition is that the area is determined by the shorter height and the width between pointers. Moving the taller pointer inward reduces width but cannot increase height because the shorter line remains limiting. Moving the shorter pointer might find a taller line, potentially increasing the area. This greedy choice ensures we don't miss the maximum area. The algorithm: compare height[left] and height[right]; if height[left] < height[right], increment left; else decrement right. If equal, we can move either pointer (commonly left). Time complexity per decision is O(1), and overall O(n) for all decisions. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the core step in the while loop. Common mistakes: moving taller pointer, not handling equal heights consistently, or using complex logic. Real code pattern: if height[left] < height[right]: left += 1 else: right -= 1.

examples.trace: Compare height[left]=3 and height[right]=5. Since 3 < 5, left is shorter. Decision: move left pointer inward to left=1.
Compare height[left]=7 and height[right]=4. Since 4 < 7, right is shorter. Decision: move right pointer inward to right=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Area Calculation with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Calculate water area between two lines given their indices and heights.
ladders.explanation: The core logic computes the area of water that can be contained between two lines at positions left and right in the array. The area is determined by the shorter of the two heights (since water will overflow over the shorter side) multiplied by the distance between them (width). Formula: area = min(height[left], height[right]) * (right - left). The intuition is that the container's height is limited by the minimum of the two lines, and width is the horizontal separation. This calculation is fundamental to evaluating potential containers. Time complexity is O(1) per calculation. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the area value used in the greedy decision to update max_area. Common mistakes: using max instead of min for height, forgetting absolute value for width, or miscalculating indices. Real code pattern: area = min(height[left], height[right]) * (right - left).

examples.trace: Height at left=4, height at right=6. Minimum height = min(4,6)=4. Width = right-left = 3-0=3. Area = 4 * 3 = 12.
Height at left=5, height at right=2. Minimum height = min(5,2)=2. Width = right-left = 5-2=3. Area = 2 * 3 = 6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Two Pointer Initialization and Movement
ladders.desc: Initialize two pointers at array ends and move them inward step by step.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves setting up two pointers at the extreme ends of an array and moving them toward each other until they meet. Initialize left pointer at index 0 and right pointer at index n-1, where n is array length. In a loop, while left < right, perform operations (like area calculation), then move pointers based on some condition (e.g., move the pointer with smaller value). The intuition is that starting from ends maximizes width initially, and moving inward explores all pairs efficiently. This pattern is common in problems requiring pairwise comparisons without O(n²) complexity. Time complexity is O(n) as each pointer moves at most n steps. Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the pointer positions for area calculation. Common mistakes: incorrect initial indices, infinite loops by not moving pointers, or moving both pointers simultaneously. Real code pattern: left, right = 0, len(arr)-1; while left < right: # do something; if condition: left += 1 else: right -= 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=3. Suppose we move left if arr[left] < arr[right] (5>1, so move right). Step 2: left=0, right=2. Compare 5 and 8 (5<8, move left). Step 3: left=1, right=2. Compare 2 and 8 (2<8, move left). Step 4: left=2, right=2, stop.
Step 1: left=0, right=2. Values equal, move left. Step 2: left=1, right=2. Values equal, move left. Step 3: left=2, right=2, stop.

testCases.input: Array [1,2,3], move left if value < 2 else right from ends
Array [4,1,5], move pointer with smaller value
Array [6,7,8,9], move right if value >7 else left
Array [5,4,3,2,1], move pointer with larger value
Array [9,8,7,6], move right if value even else left
Array [3,1,4,2], move left if index even else right

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Minimum of Two Values
ladders.desc: Determine the smaller of two given numbers.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two numbers and selecting the minimum value. Given two integers a and b, return the smaller one. If equal, return either. The intuition is that in many algorithms, decisions depend on the lesser value, such as in area calculations where the container height is limited by the shorter line. Implementation can use a simple if-else statement or a min function. Time complexity is O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L3 by being used in pointer movement decisions or area calculations. Common mistakes: incorrect comparison operators, not handling equal cases, or overcomplicating with unnecessary math. Real code pattern: min_val = a if a < b else b.

examples.trace: Compare 5 and 3. Since 3 < 5, the minimum is 3.
Compare 4 and 4. They are equal, so minimum is 4 (either value).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Pointer Variables
ladders.desc: Access array elements using indices and use variables as pointers to positions.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding arrays as ordered collections where each element has an index starting from 0. A pointer variable stores an index value, allowing us to reference specific positions in the array. For example, in an array arr = [10,20,30], arr[0] gives 10. If we set pointer i = 1, then arr[i] gives 20. Moving the pointer means changing its value, e.g., i += 1 makes i=2 so arr[i]=30. The intuition is that pointers let us traverse arrays without modifying the original data, enabling efficient algorithms. This is prerequisite for two-pointer techniques. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space is O(1) for pointer variables. This connects to L4 by providing the mechanism to access values for min operation. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in indices, confusing index with value, or not updating pointers correctly. Real code pattern: arr = [5,6,7]; i = 0; val = arr[i]; i += 1.

examples.output: Access arr[p]=3, then move p to 1
examples.trace: Initial p=0. Access arr[0] gives value 3. Update p = p + 1, so p becomes 1. Now arr[p] gives arr[1]=1.

examples.output: Access arr[q]=6, then move q to 2
examples.trace: Initial q=3. Access arr[3] gives value 6. Update q = q - 1, so q becomes 2. Now arr[q] gives arr[2]=7.

testCases.input: Array [0,0,0], pointer=1, increment pointer, new index
Array [10,20], pointer=1, decrement pointer, new index
Array [6,7,8,9], pointer=0, access then set pointer=3, access
